title: podman rm/stop: add --allow-missing flag
body:
Add an --allow-missing flag to podman rm and stop. When specified, Podman
will ignore "no such container" errors that occur when a specified
container is not present in the container store (anymore).  The
motivation behind adding this flag is to write more robust systemd
services using Podman.  A user might have manually decided to remove a
container which would lead to a failure during the `ExecStop` directive
of a systemd service referencing that container.
    
Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <rothberg@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *vrothberg* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [vrothberg] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Eeek, bash completions are missing :^)
@baude PTAL. Luckily, no changes to the API are needed but note that it's not supported on the remote client.

I really hate the flag name here.

A lot of coreutils commands like `rm` use `--force` for this, but I don't know if that makes sense here...

> I really hate the flag name here.
> 
> A lot of coreutils commands like `rm` use `--force` for this, but I don't know if that makes sense here...

`--force` currently throws an error if a specified container does not exist. Changing that behavior could break existing deployments, which is a risk I don't want to take. I initially thought about just naming it `--ignore` but thought that this might be too generic. Maybe we want to ignore more specific errors in the future, hence the `--ignore-$error-message` format.

So much for the reasoning behind the current name. I am totally open to find a different one.
@mheon @TomSweeneyRedHat @baude @giuseppe PTAL
Updated.
Personally I would go with `--ignore`, and maybe have a `-i` version.  I also think that we should have equivalent options for `podman pod stop, rm`
:cry: it's surprisingly hard to get this PR merged. Let's call it `--ignore`. I'll add it to the `pod` sub-commands as well but I hope we're done after that.
SGTM
@vrothberg a good PR is like a good food recipe.  They take a long time to gather all the great flavors before you come out with the perfect dish!  ;^)  Thanks for running with this!

> @vrothberg a good PR is like a good food recipe. They take a long time to gather all the great flavors before you come out with the perfect dish! ;^) Thanks for running with this!

I love this analogy :-) I hope we're not putting too much salt in it.
3 nits but overall LGTM. Also, tests are angry.
podman_in_podman failures look like they might not be flakes?
Tests are flaking again trying to pull images from the fedora registry
LGTM
remote prune is being painful again, restarted.
I'm currently trying to track it down. It looks like a race-condition.
one nit of a nit.  If the tests complete, I'm fine merging now and touching up later.
Good catch, @TomSweeneyRedHat ! I opened https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/4554 to tackle the flakes.
Will fix up on Monday.

I had preferred `-f` to ignore missing containers, but I see that Docker is also raising an error, so I guess we are stuck with a new flag.

Do we want to keep some freedom to extend the option in future and have something like `--ignore=noctr,...`?  I think the new flag is useful mostly for scripts, so it should not matter too much to type a few characters more.

> Do we want to keep some freedom to extend the option in future and have something like `--ignore=noctr,...`? I think the new flag is useful mostly for scripts, so it should not matter too much to type a few characters more.

That's pretty much how the PR looked initially with --ignore-no-such-container (i.e., --ignore prefix followed by the error).
/lgtm
